games.explanation: Chase Burns has been a buzzsaw against righties and a piñata against lefties, and the Yankees happen to run out a lineup built to exploit exactly that split. Ben Rice, Bellinger, and Goldschmidt all hammer right-handed pitching, and Burns has coughed up a .323 xwOBA to left-handed bats while walking nearly 12 percent of them. That is the pitch: a strikeout arm whose command and contact quality completely fall apart against the side of the plate New York stacks. Add a near-coin-flip projection against a Reds team Elo has 149 points behind, and -108 is the wrong number on the wrong side.

Tampa Bay being favored here is a fee for the logo, not a read on the actual pitching matchup. Nick Martinez has spent the year letting righties square him up with depressing regularity, and Washington happens to roll out a lineup where the dangerous bats hit from that side. Andrew Alvarez is the wild card the market keeps undervaluing, a small sample arm with a sub-.270 xwOBA against and barrel suppression that plays in any park. James Wood against a contact-dependent righty is the kind of mismatch that decides one-run games, and at +116 you are getting plus money on the better team by Elo. The board built this line off uniforms.

Webb is the kind of name that prints chalk regardless of context, and the context tonight is a Marlins lineup that happens to be built exactly wrong for him. His splits against lefties have been the soft spot in his profile all year, and Miami runs Hicks, Stowers, and Edwards at him from that side without blinking. Stowers in particular is the swing factor, a .375 xwOBA bat who turns mistake pitches into the kind of damage Webb's contact-management act can't absorb. Gusto isn't a star, but he doesn't need to be in a projected one-run game where our number says Miami wins outright more than 60% of the time. +125 on the better team is the entire pitch.

Eovaldi is the kind of name that buys a team free respect on the moneyline, and the market is treating this like a coronation rather than a tossup between two pitchers who both have real questions. Giolito isn't a problem you want to solve at home, but Texas trots out a lineup that strikes out a ton and runs cold against right-handed arms, which means this game projects to the low-scoring grind San Diego is built to win. Tatis, Machado and Merrill all carry xwOBAs north of .350 against righties, and Eovaldi has been notably more hittable to lefties anyway, so the platoon math quietly favors the dog. Getting +128 on a coin-flip the model has closer to even is the entire ballgame.
